summary: DHH addresses the uncomfortable reality that Basecamp asks job applicants to invest significant time in applications but cannot provide personalized rejection feedback. With 400+ applications for a single programmer role, spending just 20 minutes per rejection would consume nearly 7 weeks of work. He acknowledges this is unfair to applicants who invest hours in tailored cover letters and CVs, and commits to being more transparent about the process upfront so candidates can make informed decisions about whether to apply.
key_points: Basecamp's application process demands substantial time investment from candidates including custom cover letters and detailed CVs
The math of providing individual feedback at scale is prohibitive — 400 applications at 20 minutes each equals nearly 7 weeks of work
Applicants who invest hours deserve feedback but realistically will only get a generic rejection email
The solution isn't to fix the feedback problem but to be honest upfront that no feedback will be given
money_quotes: It can easily take hours to apply to a job at Basecamp.
So it's not an unreasonable expectation to hope for some detailed feedback if the application isn't successful.
And yet the math simply makes that impossible for us.

It's easily the worst part of hiring to reject hundreds of applicants who've put in a lot of their time with a generic "thank you for applying, but unfortunately we've moved forward with other candidates!".
So the least we can do is to be honest that this is the process.
key_insight: When you demand high effort from job applicants but can't reciprocate with personalized feedback, the least you owe them is upfront honesty about that asymmetry.
